text.title: mocking-strategies Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for choosing test doubles and wiring them correctly.

rule.statement: Choose the smallest double that validates the required behavior: dummy for unused params, stub for state, spy for wrapped real objects, mock only when the call is part of the contract, fake for full working replacements.
rule.rationale: Larger doubles over-specify and break on refactors; smaller doubles stay green.

rule.statement: Patch where the name is looked up, not where it is defined. If module_a imports function_b from module_b and you test module_a, patch 'module_a.function_b'.
rule.rationale: Python's name binding is per-module; patching the source module misses the test target.

rule.statement: Use AsyncMock for any function defined with `async def`. Using plain Mock returns a coroutine wrapping a Mock, not the mock's return value.
rule.rationale: Plain Mock for async functions silently breaks awaits.

rule.statement: Keep mock setup in fixtures, assertions in test functions. >5 lines of inline mock setup in a test body is a smell.
rule.rationale: Excessive inline setup signals too many collaborators or wrong layering.

rule.statement: For repository-like collaborators, prefer a fake (in-memory impl) over deep mock setup. Faster, less brittle, reads like the real code.
rule.rationale: Fakes encode the same contract once; mocks re-encode it per test.

rule.statement: Smallest double picked, patched where looked up, AsyncMock for async, fixtures for setup.
rule.rationale: Aggregate conclusion for the positive branch.

rule.statement: No collaborators (pure function) or integration test scope.
rule.rationale: Aggregate conclusion for the negative branch.
